text: In this video, we present a robotic repair of colovascular fistula due to complicated Crohn's disease.
In this video, we present a robotic repair of colovascular fistula due to complicated Crohn's disease.
The patient was a forty-seven-year-old woman who presented with abdominal pain, vomiting and fever. She reported pneumaturia during the last two weeks.

Her past medical history was significant for Crohn's disease, for which the patient was on oral mesalazine.

The surgeon mobilizes the bladder and repairs the defect using absorbable sutures, applying a two-layer closure technique.

The surgeon instills methylene blue into the bladder to visually confirm the effective closure of the repaired defect, ensuring no leakage is present.

The surgeon applies external traction to suspend the uterus, visibly elevating it within the surgical field.

The surgeon retrieves the specimen through a funnel-shaped incision and performs a transcecal anastomosis using a circular stapler.

The patient had an uneventful post-operative course and she was discharged on post-operative day five.

Final pathology revealed longitudinal fissuring ulcers extending to the serosa, consistent with Crohn's disease.
